Title: Complex evolution of novel red floral color in Petunia

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: red flower coloration associated with hummingbird pollination
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: B-ring hydroxylation branch controlling dihydroxylated versus trihydroxylated anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: developing petal limbs (stage 4; also assessed at later petal stage); higher in P. exserta than P. secreta, reduced relative to P. axillaris
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developmental stage of petal limbs; VIGS silencing
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: dihydroxylated anthocyanin precursors leading to cyanidin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: introduces 3'-hydroxylation on the flavonoid B-ring, promoting formation of dihydroxylated anthocyanins such as cyanidin derivatives
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: diverts flux toward dihydroxylated anthocyanins contributing to red hue
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: higher HT1 expression in P. exserta promotes cyanidin production and contributes to red floral color; silencing causes lighter red sectors
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HT1 expression is 4.5-fold higher in red P. exserta than in purple P. secreta. 2. Elevated HT1 shifts anthocyanin composition toward dihydroxylated cyanidin. 3. VIGS of HT1 in P. exserta produced lighter-colored sectors, supporting a role in the red hue.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; RNA-seq; allele-specific expression; VIGS; anthocyanidin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Modulating HT1 together with HF1/HF2 expression could tune cyanidin:delphinidin ratios to engineer red versus purple floral pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing caused only subtle visible effects, suggesting redundancy or compensation by F3'5'H activity.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq (NCBI SRA PRJNA674380; comparative P. inflata/P. axillaris RNA-seq PRJNA300613)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Protein sequence analysis found no unique P. exserta active-site mutations; effect is attributed mainly to expression change.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: floral color determination relevant to pollinator attraction
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: low in P. exserta petal limbs, high in P. secreta; expression divergence strengthens at later petal stage
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developmental stage of petal limbs; VIGS silencing of HF1/HF2 together
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: trihydroxylated anthocyanin precursors leading to delphinidin-type pigments
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: introduces 3' and 5' hydroxylation on the flavonoid B-ring, producing trihydroxylated anthocyanin precursors
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: reduced HF1 expression in P. exserta is proposed to be prerequisite for cyanidin accumulation and red color formation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HF1 is low in P. exserta but high in purple P. secreta. 2. Reduced HF1 relative to HT1/HF2 helps rebalance hydroxylation toward the red phenotype. 3. Combined HF silencing reduces delphinidin and alters red coloration.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; RNA-seq; VIGS of HF genes; anthocyanidin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Downregulating HF1 while maintaining HT1 can reduce excessive trihydroxylation and favor redder floral hues.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Specific function of HF1 versus HF2 could not be individually tested by VIGS due to sequence similarity.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HF1 and HF2 differ in substrate recognition sites, suggesting functional divergence.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: red flower coloration associated with hummingbird pollination
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: elevated in P. exserta petal limbs to levels similar to P. secreta; preferentially expressed over HF1 in P. exserta
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developmental stage of petal limbs; VIGS silencing of HF1/HF2 together
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: trihydroxylated anthocyanin precursors leading to delphinidin
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: catalyzes 3',5'-hydroxylation of flavonoid intermediates, supporting delphinidin-type anthocyanin synthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies delphinidin branch while coexisting with cyanidin branch in red P. exserta
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: elevated HF2 expression allows retention of delphinidin contribution within the novel red color of P. exserta
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HF2 is preferentially upregulated in P. exserta, unlike HF1. 2. Together with HT1, HF2 contributes to the mixed cyanidin/delphinidin composition underlying red color. 3. Combined HF silencing lowers delphinidin and produces lighter red sectors.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; RNA-seq; VIGS of HF genes; anthocyanidin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Selective use of HF2 with controlled HT1 expression may enable engineering of red hues that retain some delphinidin-based pigmentation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Individual HF2 contribution was not separable from HF1 in silencing assays.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Substrate recognition divergence from HF1 suggests specialized catalytic properties relevant to the phenotype.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: anthocyanins (acylated versus non-acylated forms affecting red versus purple hue)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: determines anthocyanin hue by decoration status
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: low in P. exserta petal limbs; much lower than in P. secreta; similar to P. axillaris
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developmental stage of petal limbs; VIGS silencing
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: adds acyl groups to glycosylated anthocyanins, producing decorated anthocyanins associated with purple/blue-shifted hues
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: required for storage/modification of target product; low activity prevents purple-shifted decorated anthocyanins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: downregulation of AAT in P. exserta prevents anthocyanin acylation and promotes red rather than purple hue
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAT expression is ~13.5-fold lower in P. exserta than in P. secreta. 2. P. exserta anthocyanins are strongly deficient in acylation. 3. AAT silencing in purple species causes pink sectors, demonstrating that low AAT activity shifts color toward red/pink.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; RNA-seq; allele-specific expression; VIGS; LC-MS/anthocyanidin profiling
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AAT downregulation is a practical strategy to shift purple anthocyanins toward redder hues without abolishing anthocyanin biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced acylation may also reduce downstream methylated anthocyanin formation and alter pigment stability.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AAT downregulation was considered essential for the P. exserta phenotype; protein sequence itself showed no unique disabling mutation.

Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: red floral pigmentation associated with hummingbird pollination
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: upregulated in P. exserta petal limbs; lower in P. axillaris, P. secreta, and P. inflata; shifted tissue specificity from petal veins/tube to limb
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: activates anthocyanin biosynthetic and modifying genes, restoring pathway output
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: likely functions in MBW complex with AN1/JAF13 and AN11, inferred from Petunia anthocyanin regulators
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: DPL upregulation restores anthocyanin biosynthesis in P. exserta and is required for red floral pigmentation
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: essential AN2 replacement activator enabling regain of petal limb anthocyanin production
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPL is the only SG6 MYB strongly supported as the AN2 replacement in P. exserta petal limbs. 2. VIGS of DPL causes white sectors and reduces anthocyanidins specifically in P. exserta. 3. Constitutive DPL expression in white P. axillaris restores anthocyanin biosynthesis and upregulates late anthocyanin genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; ASE; QTL colocalization; VIGS; stable overexpression (35Spro:DPL); RT-qPCR; anthocyanidin profiling
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: DPL is a strong regulatory lever for activating anthocyanin production in tissues lacking AN2 function; useful for engineering flower pigmentation, but downstream modifier genes must also be tuned for desired hue.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: 35S overexpression caused intense pigmentation in vegetative tissues and activated more downstream genes than seen in natural P. exserta, indicating pleiotropic effects and context dependence.
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV35S constitutive promoter used for functional complementation in P. axillaris
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq (NCBI SRA PRJNA674380; PRJNA300613); QTL/GBS data PRJNA704924
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developing petal limbs; VIGS; stable transgenic overexpression
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DPL acts uniquely in P. exserta; in P. hybrida it normally controls petal vein pigmentation rather than limb pigmentation.

Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: more highly expressed in P. exserta petal limbs than in P. axillaris; also elevated in P. secreta
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: anthocyanin accumulation, likely vacuolar acidification and/or transport rather than direct biosynthetic gene activation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: vacuolar P-ATPase genes in Petunia context; putative anthocyanin transport-related genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: promotes anthocyanin accumulation through a mechanism independent of direct activation of biosynthetic genes and DPL
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: shares bHLH and WD40 partners with anthocyanin MBW components in Petunia
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PH4 is essential for anthocyanin accumulation in P. exserta and P. secreta; silencing reduces anthocyanidins and causes white sectors
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: condition-specific essential regulator of pigment accumulation in the Petunia long-tube clade
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PH4 is upregulated in P. exserta and required for pigmentation in P. exserta and P. secreta. 2. PH4 silencing lowers anthocyanidins in these species, but in P. inflata mainly shifts hue toward blue. 3. PH4 does not directly activate DPL or anthocyanin biosynthetic genes in P. axillaris, implying an independent mechanism, likely transport/acidification.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RNA-seq; ASE; VIGS; transient overexpression; PH4 silencing in 35Spro:DPL background; anthocyanidin profiling
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PH4 should be co-considered with biosynthetic activators to ensure pigment accumulation/compartmentation, not just pathway gene induction.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Effects are species-context dependent; altering PH4 may change hue through vacuolar pH as well as total pigment accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV35S constitutive promoter used for transient overexpression assay
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: developing petal limbs; VIGS; transient overexpression
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper argues PH4 has a novel long-tube clade role, possibly via activating anthocyanin transport into the vacuole rather than direct biosynthetic transcription.
